coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast pore-tailoring of dense microporous carbon for high volumetric performance supercapacitors in organic electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiqi
Jiang, Yuting
Jiang, Zimu
Zhu, Jiayao
Gan, Xuemeng
Qin, Fuwei
Tang, Tingting
Luo, Wanxia
Guo, Nannan
Liu, Zheng
Wang, Luxiang
Zhang, Su
Jia, Dianzeng
Fan, Zhuangjun
coredata.dc:description: Preparing activated carbon with high volumetric performance in organic electrolyte is crucial for developing high-energy and miniaturized supercapacitors, but still challenging because high surface area and large pore size are required to effectively store large-sized organic electrolyte ions, which usually result in the low density. Herein, an ultrafast strategy is proposed to tailor the interconnected porous structure of activated carbon through introducing CO2 at the end of homogeneous activation. The uniformly distributed potassium components in carbon matrix promote a robust catalytic etching by CO2, leading to precise tailoring of the ultramicropores (0.50–0.72 nm) to large micropores (0.81–1.14 nm) within only 2 min. The optimized sample with both high surface area and compaction density (2147 m2 g−1, 0.68 g cm−3) shows high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances (155 F g−1 and 106 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) as well as good rate performance (102 F g−1 and 70 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1) in organic electrolyte. The symmetric supercapacitor achieves high energy densities of 35 Wh kg−1 and 24 Wh L−1 at 573 W kg−1 and 395 W L−1, respectively, indicating great potential of the ultrafast tailoring strategy for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Activated carbon
Ultrafast pore-tailoring
Volumetric capacitance
Rate performance
Supercapacitors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ultrafast pore-tailoring of TPCs through introducing CO2 at the end of homogeneous activation for a few minutes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (b) pore size distributions, (c) porous structure evolution and compaction densities, (d) surface areas (SBET) and potential surface areas (SD > 0.67 nm) of TPCs and (e) scheme of the porous structure evolution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HRTEM images of (a, b) TPC-0 and (c, d) TPC-2. (e) XRD patterns and (f) Raman spectra of the TPCs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance of TPCs and commercial activated carbon (YP–50F): (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at 1 A g−1, (c) gravimetric capacitance, (d) volumetric capacitance, and (e) Nyquist plots (the inset shows contact resistance (Rc) and electrical double-layer formation impedance (Ref)). (f) Gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of TPC-2 compared with other reported carbon materials. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance of TPC-2//TPC-2 and YP-50F//YP-50F symmetric supercapacitors with 10 mg cm−2 electrode mass loading in the voltage window of 0–2.7 V: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at 1 A g−1, (c) rate performance, and (d) Ragone plots. (e) Cycle performance with the mass loading of 2 mg cm−2 at 10 A g−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Ultrafast pore-tailoring of dense microporous carbon for high volumetric performance supercapacitors in organic electrolyte

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors with equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparing activated carbon with high volumetric performance in organic electrolyte is crucial for developing high-energy and miniaturized supercapacitors, but still challenging because high surface area and large pore size are required to effectively store large-sized organic electrolyte ions, which usually result in the low density. Herein, an ultrafast strategy is proposed to tailor the interconnected porous structure of activated carbon through introducing CO2 at the end of homogeneous activation. The uniformly distributed potassium components in carbon matrix promote a robust catalytic etching by CO2, leading to precise tailoring of the ultramicropores (0.50–0.72 nm) to large micropores (0.81–1.14 nm) within only 2 min. The optimized sample with both high surface area and compaction density (2147 m2 g−1, 0.68 g cm−3) shows high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances (155 F g−1 and 106 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) as well as good rate performance (102 F g−1 and 70 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1) in organic electrolyte. The symmetric supercapacitor achieves high energy densities of 35 Wh kg−1 and 24 Wh L−1 at 573 W kg−1 and 395 W L−1, respectively, indicating great potential of the ultrafast tailoring strategy for practical application.

Introducing CO2 at the end of homogeneous activation for only 2 min effectively tailors the porous structure of activated carbon, leading to high gravimetric and volumetric capacitance as well as robust kinetics for supercapacitors in organic electrolytes.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para:
Supercapacitors have attracted widespread attention for high-power supplies such as electric vehicles, smart grids, and brake energy recycling because of their fast charge-discharge rate, long cycle life, and high safety [1–5]. However, the crucial obstacle restricting their practical application is the low energy densities of 5–10 Wh kg−1 or 5–8 Wh L−1, which is two orders of magnitudes lower than those of lithium ion batteries (about 150 Wh kg−1, 250 Wh L−1) [6–8]. According to the equation
E = 1 / 2
C
V 2
, high working voltage window, generally controlled by the utilized electrolytes, is one of the key factors to ensure high energy density of supercapacitors [9,10]. Although aqueous electrolytes with high safety, low-cost, and good ion conductivity are widely investigated for high-rate supercapacitors in laboratories, the low voltage window determined by thermodynamic water decomposition (1.23 V) severely restricts the energy density [11,12]. Therefore, capacitive energy storage using organic electrolytes and activated carbon still dominate the market of commercial supercapacitors.

At present, commercial activated carbons with large surface area over 2400 m2 g−1 can achieve high gravimetric capacitance over 100 F g−1 in organic electrolytes [13–16]. With the future development of low-cost, miniaturization, and lightweight in devices, the volumetric capacitance (C
v ) is more reliable to present the true performance than gravimetric capacitance (C
g ), especially for devices which should be installed in limited space [17–19]. According to the equation C
v
= C
g
× ρ, large-surface-area activated carbon usually shows limited volumetric performance due to its low density (0.4–0.6 g cm−3). More seriously, as organic ions have large diameters, large-sized pores are required to accommodate and transport these ions, hindering the development of high-volumetric-performance activated carbon for compact energy storage.

Activated carbon shows a primary electrical double-layer behavior in organic electrolytes [20–24]. Therefore, design suitable porous structure to balance large surface area, large pore size, and high density is the main strategy to improve the volumetric capacitance. For instance, carbide-derived carbons with well-controlled microporous structure have been prepared through chlorine etching of the ball-milled TiC, showing an improved volumetric capacitance of 74 F cm−3 in NEt4BF4/AN electrolyte [25]. Using kinds of graphene (graphene nanosheets, graphene oxide, holey graphene etc.) as the building blocks, several high-density carbon materials with controllable porous structure or interlayer spacing have been prepared through nonvolatile electrolyte-pillar [26], layer-by-layer assembly [27,28], mechanical compression [29,30], and capillary-densification [31–33], performing high volumetric capacitance and good rate performance in organic and/or ionic liquid electrolytes. Activated carbon have been widely used in commercial supercapacitors [34,35]. But most of them are prepared by an outside-to-inside activation using carbon blocks as precursors, resulting in an uncontrollable branched porous structure and low density. Previously, secondary activation (treating activated carbon in air, CO2, or steam atmospheres) has been used to modulate the specific surface area and pore size distribution of activated carbons (Table S1). However, these works usually took more than 1-h treatment, causing a severe pore size expansion and further density decrease. Recently, Yang et al. proposed a densification strategy to pack activated carbon into a compact graphene network. The obtained sample exhibited an improved volumetric capacitance of 138 F cm−3 in an ionic liquid electrolyte [32]. However, improving the density, volumetric capacitance, and rate performance of activated carbon from a pore-engineering aspect during preparation is still challenging.

Herein, we propose an ultrafast pore-tailoring strategy to integrate large surface area, large pore size, and high density in activated carbon during preparation (Fig. 1 ). We have prepared an interconnected ultramicroporous carbon by homogeneous activation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using the uniformly mixed KOH [36]. By introducing CO2 at the end of activation for only 2 min, the porous structure of the sample is effectively tailored from ultramicropores (0.50–0.72 nm) to large micropores (0.81–1.14 nm) and some small mesopores benefiting from robust catalytic effect of the potassium (K) components. The enlarged pore sizes are suitable for accommodation and transportation of large-diameter organic ions. Besides, the highly crystallized GQDs help to improve the electrochemical kinetics by forming entire conductive networks. Therefore, the optimized sample shows both high surface area of 2147 m2 g−1 and density of 0.68 g cm−3, achieving high gravimetric/volumetric capacitances (155 F g−1/106 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) as well as good rate performance (66% capacitance retention at 20 A g−1) in organic electrolyte. The assembled symmetric supercapacitor at commercial electrode mass loading of 10 mg cm−2 shows high energy densities of 35 Wh kg−1/24 Wh L−1 at 573 W kg−1/395 W L−1, indicating great potential of the ultrafast pore-tailoring strategy for practical application.

GQDs are prepared by chemical oxidation using bituminous coal powder (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) as the precursor. The detailed synthesis procedure is available in our previous studies [37,38]. Commercial activated carbon (Kuraray YP-50F) was purchased from Shenzhen Kejing Star Technology Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, Guangdong, China).

In a typical process, 0.5 g GQDs and 0.25 g KOH were thoroughly dispersed into 40 mL ethanol by vigorous stirring and sonication. After being dried at 80 °C, the mixture was milled to fine powder, and then mechanically compressed in a pressure mold (inner diameter of 10 mm) under 10 MPa and kept for a few minutes. Afterwards, the obtained thin tablets were put into a tubular furnace (diameter: 5 cm, length: 100 cm) and carbonized at 800 °C for 1 h under N2 flow protection (80 mL min−1) with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Once the activation time reached 1 h, the N2 flow was directly changed to CO2 flow of 80 mL min−1 and kept at 800 °C for certain minutes. Thereafter, the CO2 flow was changed back to N2 flow during cooling down to room temperature. The obtained block was milled to fine powder, immersed into 1 mol L−1 HCl solution to remove the inorganic salts, and then washed to neutral using deionized water. The samples were named as TPC-x, in where “x” referred to the time of CO2 introduction (minutes).

For comparison, the homogeneously activated porous carbon (TPC-0) was prepared by the same method but without CO2 treatment [36]. To understand the influence of K components on the pore-tailoring, TPC-0 (after removal of the K components by acid washing) was heated to 800 °C in N2 atmosphere, and treated by CO2 flow for 2 min by the same procedure. The sample was named as TPC-RK-2, where “RK” represented removal of K components by acid washing.

The morphology and structure of the obtained samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F). The crystal structure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). Raman spectrum was examined on a Bruker SENTERRA dispersive Raman spectrometer with a wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer. The samples were characterized by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at −196 °C using a volumetric adsorption apparatus (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics). The surface area was obtained by Braunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The pore size distribution was calculated by Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method.

The electrochemical performance was measured by a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China) using 1 mol L−1 tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate salt (TEABF4) solution in propylene carbonate (PC) as the electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared by mixing the obtained sample, carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binder with a weight ratio of 85: 10: 5 in ethanol to form a slurry, coated onto stainless steel mesh current collector, and subsequently vacuum dried at 110 °C for 10 h. The cellulose separator in the form of TF4035 type from Japan NKK company was used to assemble two-electrode symmetric supercapacitors in a CR2032 cell type. The intrinsic electrochemical performance was measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge in the potential window of 0–2.5 V to avoid the possible polarization. The power/energy densities were measured in the potential window of 0–2.7 V. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted with the frequency range from 0.01 to 100 kHz by means of applying alternating current amplitude of 5 mV. Gravimetric capacitance (C
g , F g−1) and volumetric capacitance (C
v , F cm−3) of the electrode materials were calculated from the charge-discharge curves according to the equations:
(1)
C g
= I Δ t / m Δ V
where I is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the active mass loading on the single electrode (g), ΔV is the voltage window (V).

The volumetric capacitance (C
v ) was evaluated according to the following equations:
(2)
C v
=
C g
× ρ
(3)
ρ = m / V
where C
g (F g−1) is gravimetric capacitance, ρ (g cm−3) is the compaction density of active material, m (g) and V (cm3) are certain mass and the volume of active material. We calculated the volume of active material by measuring the radius (r) and thickness (h) of the compressed active material under the pressure of 10 MPa in a cylinder mold (V = h∗π∗r
2 ). According to the equation:

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) were calculated by the following equations:
(4)
E = C
( Δ V )
2
/ 8 × 3.6
(5)
P = 3600 E / Δ t
where C is the gravimetric/volumetric capacitance of the symmetric supercapacitor calculated based on the total mass of the electrodes, ΔV is the voltage window (V) excluding the IR drop, and Δt is the discharge time (s).

Our previous work showed that when GQDs were uniformly mixed with small amount of KOH through ion exchange, the subsequent homogeneous activation led to formation of the interconnected ultramicroporous carbon [36,39]. As seen from the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and the pore size distribution (Fig. 2 a and b), TPC-0 shows a type I isotherm consisting of a rapid adsorption in the low pressure region (P/P0 < 0.1) and a small hysteresis loop in the medium pressure region (0.5< P/P0 < 0.9), indicating a micropore-dominated structure with the pore size concentrated at 0.50 and 0.72 nm as well as a small amount of mesopores centered at 2.10 nm (Fig. 2b). The ultramicroporous structure enables TPC-0 with large surface area of 1730 m2 g−1 and high compaction density of 0.97 g cm−3, which are suitable for compact energy storage in aqueous electrolyte [36], but fail to effectively accommodate and transport organic ions because of their large ion sizes (for example, the desolvated TEA+ and BF4 ¯ ions are 0.67 and 0.48 nm, respectively) [11,12,40].

To tailor the porous structure as well as maintain the high density, we introduced CO2 gas at the end of homogeneous activation for a few minutes, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Even 1 min CO2 introduction leads to an enlargement of the micropore size to 0.51, 0.81, 1.14, and 2.18 nm for TPC-1 (Fig. 2b). The significantly increased surface area (2124 m2 g−1, Fig. 2c and d) and micro- and meso-pore volumes (0.76 and 0.26 cm3 g−1) of TPC-1 demonstrate a secondary activation process by etching the amorphous regions of the carbon skeleton (Fig. 2e), as shown in the XRD and Raman results below. Thereafter, the sharp micropore distribution is broadened for TPC-2, covering the region from 0.54 to 1.14 nm. It is realized that although some micropores are converted to mesopores, slightly increased total pore volume (from 1.02 to 1.21 cm3 g−1) leads to an increased surface area to 2147 m2 g−1 for TPC-2 due to moderate CO2 etching. Therefore, pore size enlargement in this process is accompanied by merging of the micropores due to severe etching of the pore walls. Further increase the CO2 introduction time to 3 min leads to not only the pore size enlargement, but also formation of some new micropores at ca. 0.51 nm as well as increased surface area to 2530 m2 g−1 for TPC-3 due to the excessive activation. According to the desolvated TEA+ diameter, we define the potential surface area (SD > 0.67 nm) contributed by the pores with a diameter larger than 0.67 nm [40,41]. Obviously, enlarging the pore size increases the SD > 0.67 nm from 675 of TPC-0 to 1536 and 1912 m2 g−1 of TPC-2 and TPC-3, respectively (Fig. 2d and Table S2). However, increasing pore volume and enlarging pore size cause the decreased compaction densities from 0.97 to 0.63 g cm−3 (Fig. 2c). Besides, the strong CO2 etching results in a low yield when prolonging the CO2 introduction time to more than 3 min (lower than 15 wt %, Table S3). Therefore, large surface area, suitable pore size, high density, and high yield should be balanced to obtain the optimized TPC.

It should be realized that we introduced CO2 at the end of homogeneous activation, the uniformly distributed K components in the samples could play important influences for CO2 etching [42]. For deep understanding, we treated TPC-0 (after removal of K components by acid washing) by the same CO2 treatment for 2 min to prepare TPC-RK-2. Interestingly, TPC-RK-2 shows a slightly increased surface area of 1831 m2 g−1 and micropore-dominated structure similar to TPC-0 (Fig. S1). The quite different results of containing/removing K components for pore-tailoring demonstrate that K components indeed play crucial roles for promoting the CO2 etching [42] (Fig. S2). Unfortunately, the composition and distribution of the K components are hardly measured because of their high chemical reactivity in air (some K substance is formed, which will burn in air).

Previously, we showed that when GQDs were mixed with small amount of KOH, K+ was immobilized by oxygen-bearing functional groups and uniformly distributed in the precursor, leading to the formation of interconnected porous structure after homogeneous activation [39]. Therefore, it is reasonable to deduce that the as-formed K components are also uniformly located at the micropores. When CO2 is introduced at the end of homogeneous activation, the highly reactive K components (such as K and K2O) [43–48] combine with CO2 and subsequently react with carbon skeleton rapidly, as shown in reactions (6–9). Therefore, the K components act as catalysts to promote the occurrence and circulation of catalytic reaction through the role of oxygen transfer, as shown in the global reaction (10).
(6)
2 K + C
O 2
→
K 2
O + C O
(7)
K 2
O + C → C O + 2 K
(8)
K 2
O + C
O 2
→
K 2
C
O 3
(9)
K 2
C
O 3
+ C →
K 2
O + 2 C O
(10)
C + C
O 2
→
[ K ]
2 C O

All of the obtained TPCs present a block morphology with the average size of ca. 2 μm (Fig. S3), indicating that CO2 treatment mainly tailors the porous structure. TPC-0 shows a dense and highly disordered structure (Fig. 3 a) with a large number of ultramicropores formed by homogeneous activation, as shown in the HRTEM image [49] (Fig. 3b). By contrast, TPC-2 exhibits a relatively loose structure with some uniformly distributed small mesopores (marked by white circles in Fig. 3c), which is in well accordance with the N2 adsorption-desorption results. Moreover, apart from the micropores, many highly crystallized domains (marked by the white regions) around small mesopores are observed from the zoomed image in Fig. 3d, suggesting the improved crystallinity of TPCs after CO2 catalytic etching. As seen from the XRD patterns (Fig. 3e), two diffraction peaks located at approximately 23° and 43° correspond to the (002) and (100) crystal plane of carbon, respectively [50]. TPC-0 shows an amorphous nature with no obvious diffraction peaks. For other CO2 treated samples, an obvious increase in the low-angle scattering region is due to the formation of abundant small mesopores [51,52]. It is also noticed that after CO2 treatment, the crystallinity first increases from TPC-0 to TPC-2, then slightly decreases to TPC-3, as revealed by the variation of (002) peak intensities. In accordance with the XRD patterns, the peak area ratio of D and G bands (AD/AG) in Raman spectra represents the defect density of carbon materials [53,54]. TPC-2 also shows the lowest AD/AG value and defect density than the others (Figs. 3f and S4). Appropriate CO2 treatment can improve the crystallinity and reduce the defect density of TPCs because the highly-reactive amorphous regions are preferentially removed away during catalytic etching, which is beneficial for improving the electrical conductivity of the samples.

Interestingly, TPC-1 with moderate crystallinity and defect density shows the highest electrical conductivity of 103 S m−1. Prolonging the CO2 treating time results in a decreased conductivity of TPC-2 (76 S m−1) and TPC-3 (61 S m−1) (Fig. S5). Considering the similar chemical compositions of TPCs from the XPS spectra (ca. 89 at. % C, 10 at. % O, and 1 at. % N, Fig. S6 and Table S4), the decreased conductivity is because formation of abundant mesopores by CO2 etching breaks some of the electron transport paths. Nevertheless, the highly crystallized GQD precursor ensures higher electrical conductivity of TPCs than the commercial activated carbon (YP–50F, 39 S m−1), contributing to good electrochemical kinetics and rate performance.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at 10 mV s−1 and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at 1 A g−1 of all the samples present highly rectangular and triangular shapes at a voltage window of 0–2.5 V, respectively, indicating an electrical double-layer behavior (Fig. 4 a and b). Although TPC-0 without CO2 treatment shows a large surface area of 1730 m2 g−1, many ultramicropores (D < 0.67 nm) cannot effectively accommodate large-sized organic ions, resulting in low specific capacitance of 115 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. With the short-time introduction of CO2, the porous structure of TPC-2 is expanded, leading to SD > 0.67 nm increase from 675 to 1536 m2 g−1, which is suitable for the storage and transfer of large-sized organic ions. As shown in Figs. 4c and S7, TPC-2 shows the best capacitance performance of 155 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 102 F g−1 at 20 A g−1, higher than those of TPC-1 (151 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 71 F g−1 at 20 A g−1), TPC-3 (153 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 81 F g−1 at 20 A g−1) and YP-50F activated carbon (90 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 26 F g−1 at 20 A g−1). It is worth noting that enlarged pore size by introducing CO2 endows TPCs with increased SD > 0.67 nm, which is beneficial for improving capacitive energy storage. It is also realized that increasing CO2 treating time to 3 min endows TPC-3 with the largest SD > 0.67 nm of 1912 m2 g−1 (calculated by ions diameter). But excessive CO2 etching results in formation of new micropores and decreased conductivity (61 S m−1). Therefore, although TPC-3 shows the highest SD > 0.67 nm, some surface and space cannot be efficiently utilized due to blocked conductive paths, resulting in lower rate performance than TPC-2. Furthermore, the compaction densities of TPCs decrease from 0.97 to 0.63 g cm−3 with the increase of CO2 treating time (Fig. 2c). Owing to the moderate density of 0.68 g cm−3 and high capacitances, TPC-2 yields high volumetric capacitances of 106 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and 70 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1 (Fig. 4d). As a result, although the gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of TPC-2 are lower than those of some graphene-based materials (150–300 F g−1, 100–250 F cm−3), they are superior to commercial activated carbons (80–100 F g−1, 50–70 F cm−3) and most of the reported activated carbons (Fig. 4f) [22,23,25,26,55–63].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to reveal the electrochemical kinetics of the TPCs (Fig. 4e). As there is no charge-transfer process (electrochemical reactions) for an electrical double-layer capacitor energy storage, for clear understanding, the “Ref” is defined as the impedance of electrical double-layer formation in our work. The impedance curve ranging from the starting point of the semicircle to the terminal point of 45° line is composed of three parts: (1) contact resistance (Rc) in high frequency region, (2) electrical double-layer formation impedance (Ref), and (3) in-pore diffusion resistance (Rd) in middle frequency region [64,65]. In the simulated Nyquist plots by the equivalent circuit model (Figs. S8 and S9), the values of Rc fluctuate in the very low range of 15% due to the personal electrode preparation techniques. The simulated Ref and Rd values first decrease from TPC-0 to TPC-2, then slightly increase to TPC-3 (Table S5). This demonstrates that enlarged pore size by proper CO2 treatment not only increases SD > 0.67 nm, but also improves the electrical double-layer formation and the ion migration kinetics. However, over-etching by prolonging CO2 treating time to 3 min results in the destroyed conductive path and increased Ref and Rd. The tails in the low frequency region of TPC-2 become more vertical than the other samples, demonstrating the best capacitive behavior. By the way, although the electrode was mechanically compacted under the pressure of 10 MPa, the electrolyte infiltration and ion transportation are still good enough for sustaining good energy storage performance. The reason is due to the pores supported by carbon black particles in the electrode, as proved in Figs. S10, S11, and S12. Therefore, the outstanding electrochemical performance of TPC-2 is attributed to: (1) enlarged pore size by suitable CO2 treatment and good wettability of electrolyte are in favor of robust ion transport kinetics and (2) high conductivity (76 S m−1) from improved crystallinity and continuous electron transport paths ensure sufficient surface area utilization (Fig. S5).

To evaluate the potential for practical application, the electrochemical performance of TPC-2//TPC-2 symmetric supercapacitor was assembled with the commercial level electrode mass loading of 10 mg cm−2. From the CV and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves, no polarization is observed at the commonly used voltage window of 0–2.7 V (Fig. 5 a). At this mass loading, TPC-2//TPC-2 symmetric supercapacitor shows slightly decreased capacitive and rate performance (41 F g−1/28 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1, 10 F g−1/7 F cm−3 at 10 A g−1), but still much better than YP-50F//YP-50F activated carbon (Figs. 5c and S13). The energy densities of TPC-2//TPC-2 symmetric supercapacitor is 35 Wh kg−1 and 24 Wh L−1 at the power densities of 573 W kg−1 and 395 W L−1, which is higher than or comparable with YP-50F//YP-50F supercapacitor and other reported symmetric supercapacitors at similar electrode mass loadings in organic electrolytes [54,55,66] (Fig. 5d). Moreover, the device shows good cycle stability with 95.8% capacitance retention after 20,000 charge/discharge cycles at 10 A g−1 and maintains a Coulombic efficiency of 100% during cycling (Fig. 5e). The small equivalent series resistance (ESR) increment again indicate superb cycle stability (Fig. S14 and Table S6), revealing the great potential for practical applications.

In summary, we have demonstrated an ultrafast strategy to tailor interconnected porous structure of activated carbon by introducing CO2 at the end of homogeneous activation for only 2 min. Owing to robust catalytic effect of the potassium components, the porous structure of the sample is effectively tailored from ultramicropores (0.50–0.72 nm) to large micropores (0.81–1.14 nm) and some small mesopores, which are suitable for accommodation and transportation of large-diameter organic ions. Therefore, the optimized sample with high surface area (2147 m2 g−1), enlarged potential surface area (from 675 to 1536 m2 g−1), and high density (0.68 g cm−3) shows high gravimetric/volumetric capacitance of 155 F g−1/106 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 as well as good rate performance of 102 F g−1/70 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1 in organic electrolyte. Moreover, the assembled symmetric supercapacitor at commercial electrode mass loading of 10 mg cm−2 shows high energy density of 35 Wh kg−1/24 Wh L−1 at 573 W kg−1/395 W L−1. Our work may provide a new thought for preparing dense activated carbon from pore-engineering aspect.

Qiqi Li: Experiment operation, Writing – original draft. Yuting Jiang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Zimu Jiang: Physical characterizations, Methodology. Jiayao Zhu: Experiment operation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Xuemeng Gan: Experiment operation, Formal analysis. Fuwei Qin: Experiment operation, Methodology. Tingting Tang: Formal analysis. Wanxia Luo: Methodology. Nannan Guo: Formal analysis. Zheng Liu: Material characterization. Luxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Draft revision, Supervision. Dianzeng Jia: Formal analysis, Methodology. Zhuangjun Fan: Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision, All authors contributed to the scientific discussion and manuscript revisions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046, 51702275, 51972342, and 51872056), the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (ts20190922), Key Basic Research Projects of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong province (ZR2019ZD51), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (20CX05010A), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).
